blueprint_refs: STRICT: "Pattern/Persona/Motivation must be computed using only days strictly before each target date."
"For each target (validation) day, compute Motivation features strictly from the resident's past 7 observed days prior to the target date"

evidence.metrics: validation metrics are strong overall (topk_poi_recall=0.92), but current implementation can still violate per-target pre-date conditioning when target_day is inside the training set.

validation-pattern-leakage-uses-train-on.error_identification: Blueprint requires per-target-day Pattern/Persona/Motivation computed strictly from days < target day. Current calibration/training rollout uses agent.available_training_days=train_by_user and then ensure_pattern_upto(day) filters within that set; but when evaluating training days, the cutoff is within the same train set and should exclude future training days after the cutoff. The code currently does exclude days >= cutoff in ensure_pattern_upto, but Motivation uses the same source and is only neutral if <7 prior days; this can still mismatch the Blueprint’s ‘past 7 observed days prior to target day’ requirement if the per-target context should include ALL observed days prior to that training target, not just the preselected 80% training split.
validation-pattern-leakage-uses-train-on.root_cause_analysis: Design decision conflates two different contexts: (1) per-user train/val split, and (2) per-target pre-date context. In BaseCalibrator.fit and RandomSearchCalibrator.fit, agent.available_training_days is set to split.train_by_user[uid] (earliest 80% dates). For any target day inside that set, the Blueprint’s pre-target context should be ‘all observed days before that date’ (still within 2021), not restricted to the train split subset. This affects both ResidentAgent.ensure_pattern_upto() and MobilitySimulator._compute_motivation().
validation-pattern-leakage-uses-train-on.correct_approach: During calibration rollouts over training days, set the per-target context source to the user’s full 2021 observed history (agent.observed_2021), but enforce that the *loss* uses only training targets. Concretely: do not overwrite agent.available_training_days with train_by_user for calibration; instead add a separate guard that prevents using validation targets as rollouts, while allow ensure_pattern_upto(day) to see any observed day < day (including those that would be in validation if they are earlier than the current training target). Keep the validation rollout as-is (where only days < validation target exist by definition).
validation-pattern-leakage-uses-train-on.key_insight: Keep ‘which days are targets for optimization’ separate from ‘which past days are allowed as conditioning context’. Train/val split is about targets; pre-target conditioning is about time ordering.

blueprint_refs: "sample baseline number of stops ... then ... apply Motivation ..."
"Decrease with total time ... influences probability to return home/end day."
stop-count-underprediction-and-early-ter.error_identification: Stop counts are still noticeably off (mean absolute error ~0.73 stops/day; KL ~0.36), indicating systematic bias rather than noise.
stop-count-underprediction-and-early-ter.root_cause_analysis: generate_day() samples a target stop_count, but then reduces realized stops via: (a) feasibility clamp using min_gap=5 and remaining_budget (reasonable), (b) a late-day stopping hazard p_stop that can trigger once len(visits)>=2, and (c) hard break when t_next>1439. This creates a consistent under-shoot of the sampled stop_count, and the hazard is only time/progress-based (not calibrated against the per-user stop_count_hist or last-visit-time statistics).
stop-count-underprediction-and-early-ter.correct_approach: Make realized stop count consistent with the sampled stop_count unless the day truly runs out of time: remove/disable the stochastic early-stop hazard for most of the day, or calibrate it to match the empirical probability of ending before completing planned stops. Practical patch: only allow early termination once (t >= 20:00) AND (len(visits) >= stop_count-1), or compute p_stop from remaining_time vs expected dwell+travel so it triggers mainly when infeasible. Then re-check stop_count_kl/abs error.
stop-count-underprediction-and-early-ter.key_insight: If you sample a discrete budget (stops), don’t also add an uncalibrated termination process that systematically truncates the budget; tie termination to feasibility or fit it to data.

blueprint_refs: "trip distance distributions (computed from consecutive-stop geographic distances)"

code_refs.symbol: Evaluator._trip_distances + load_data (adding missing POIs without coords) + MobilitySimulator._distance_km_or_fallback

trip-distance-metric-low-coverage-and-fa.error_identification: Trip-distance evaluation is based on only ~30% of legs having coordinates (gt coord_match_rate≈0.30; sim≈0.29). This makes trip_distance_wasserstein less informative and can hide distance model problems or induce unstable calibration behavior.
trip-distance-metric-low-coverage-and-fa.root_cause_analysis: load_data() adds many trajectory-only POIs with lat/lon=None (stdout: added 3858). Simulator chooses POIs without requiring coords; when coords are missing, generation uses sampled fallback distances for timing/utility, but evaluation ignores those legs entirely. Even with the enforced coord-known share in _choose_next_poi, sim coord_match_rate remains ~0.29.
trip-distance-metric-low-coverage-and-fa.correct_approach: Increase coordinate match rate beyond ~0.3 by tightening candidate selection when coordinates are available: in _choose_next_poi(), when there exists any coord-known POI in the category, restrict pool to coord-known POIs (or set min_coord_share closer to 0.85–0.95), and only fall back to coord-unknown if the category has no coords. This directly increases matched_pairs_with_coords in Evaluator._trip_distances, improving the signal and typically reducing trip_distance_wasserstein variance.
trip-distance-metric-low-coverage-and-fa.key_insight: If a metric is computed on a joinable subset (needs coordinates), ensure the simulator generates joinable outputs; otherwise the metric becomes a low-power, biased diagnostic.

blueprint_refs: "Encounter probability can be a function of POI coarse category (if available)."
"awareness boost ... additive term to category utility and decays over time"

evidence.metrics: topk_poi_recall=0.92, transition_divergence=0.0234 (good), but current mechanism can over-amplify some categories depending on mapping collisions

category-choice-awareness-double-countin.error_identification: Encounter-driven awareness is added to both fine_cat and coarse category keys during rollout, and then _choose_next_category applies awareness by checking fine first and then coarse. This can effectively stack boosts when fine==coarse or when both keys are present for the same semantic category.
category-choice-awareness-double-countin.root_cause_analysis:
In rollout(), for each encounter POI the code does:
- ag.category_awareness[fine_cat] += 0.05
- ag.category_awareness[coarse] += 0.05
Then in _choose_next_category() it reads:
aw = agent.category_awareness.get(c, agent.category_awareness.get(coarse, 0.0))
This mixes levels inconsistently: if c is already coarse (because categories are not mapped consistently), the boost can be applied in unintended ways.
category-choice-awareness-double-countin.correct_approach: Choose one representation for awareness (prefer coarse categories, since Blueprint suggests coarse-category dependence). Patch: only store awareness under coarse category, and in _choose_next_category() always lookup by to_coarse_category(c). If you need fine-grained, store fine only and never also store coarse for the same event.
category-choice-awareness-double-countin.key_insight: Don’t store the same signal at multiple taxonomy levels unless you also enforce a non-overlapping lookup rule; otherwise you create hidden amplification.
